coredata.dc:title: Role of Pyridinic-N for Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in oxygen reaction reduction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lang
Luo, Yi
Li, Ming
Hu, Guanghui
Xu, Yongjie
Tang, Tao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibit exciting properties in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) for ample electrocatalytic edging and N-doped active sites. However, low yield and high dispersity of N-GQDs prohibit their direct application as the electrocatalyst. In this paper, two facile hydrothermal progress were developed to synthesize the high-yield N-GQDs with the diameter of ca. 2–6nm and the hybrid of N-GQDs/Reduced Graphene Oxide (N-GQDs/r-GO). The results demonstrated that the N-GQDs/r-GO display remarkable electrocatalytic activity. Moreover, it can be found that the pyridinic-N plays a major role in ORR. Both the average electron transfer number and the onset potential depend on the content of pyridinic-N. The proposed synthesis strategy is facile and low-cost, serving as a feasible method for the development of highly efficient electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped graphene quantum dots
Oxygen reduction reaction
Pyridinic-N
Reduced graphene oxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The FE-TEM image of N-GQDs/r-GO-250, (b) the HRTEM image of N-GQDs.
(a) The survey XPS spectrums of GQDs, N-GQDs-50, N-GQDs-200, N-GQDs-250. (b) The peak of deconvolution of N1s core level of N-GQDs-250.

(a) The CV curves of N-GQDs/r-GO-250 on a GC electrode in N2 or O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. (b) The LSV of samples at s sweep rate of 5mV/s at 1600rpm in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. (c) The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of N-GQDs/r-GO-250 with different rotation rates from 400 to 2500rpm (Inset, Koutecky-Levich plots of N-GQDs/r-GO-250 derived from −0.6 to −0.9V). (d) The electron transfer number (n) of N-GQDs/r-GO-50, N-GQDs/r-GO-200, N-GQDs/r-GO-250.

(a) The distributions of the two N types for the N-GQDs obtained with different volumes of ammonia in the reaction process. (b) The dependence of the average electron transfer number on ammonia volumes. (c) The dependence of the average electron transfer number on pyridinic-N content.
The contents of doped-N and the relative ratios of different N species.

head.ce:title: Role of Pyridinic-N for Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in oxygen reaction reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with different pyridinic-N contents modified reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) catalysts demonstrated the electrocatalytic activities and can thus be used as metal-free catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline media. pyridinic-N played an important part in electrocatalytic ORR, wherein N-GQD/r-GO samples with higher pyridinic-N content markedly increased electrocatalytic property by promoting onset potential, cathodic reduction peak, and current density.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) exhibit exciting properties in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) for ample electrocatalytic edging and N-doped active sites. However, low yield and high dispersity of N-GQDs prohibit their direct application as the electrocatalyst. In this paper, two facile hydrothermal progress were developed to synthesize the high-yield N-GQDs with the diameter of ca. 2–6nm and the hybrid of N-GQDs/Reduced Graphene Oxide (N-GQDs/r-GO). The results demonstrated that the N-GQDs/r-GO display remarkable electrocatalytic activity. Moreover, it can be found that the pyridinic-N plays a major role in ORR. Both the average electron transfer number and the onset potential depend on the content of pyridinic-N. The proposed synthesis strategy is facile and low-cost, serving as a feasible method for the development of highly efficient electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past several years, serious energy crisis encouraged us to explore clean, low-cost energy to gratify global energy needs [1]. Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) plays a vital role in fuel cells and metal–air batteries [2,3]. Therefore, discovery of sustainable electrocatalysts is benefit to the entire energy industry [4]. In the past several decades, numerous aspects of platinum-based materials, which are commercially used as catalysts, were investigated because of their excellent electrocatalytic activity and low over-potential to obtain a certain current [5]. However, platinum is a scarce element and intolerant to methanol. Catalytic activity of platinum is decreased during ORR. These drawbacks hinder application of this element in fuel cells and metal–air batteries. Therefore, exploration of superior performance catalysts arouses considerable interest from both industrial and academic researchers [6,7].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to new members of carbon materials with zero-dimensional structure and sizes within 10nm and are researched in terms of synthesis, doping, and application [8]. Especially, GQDs feature ample edge sites that contribute to electrocatalytic activity, often reacting at edge sites of extra materials compared with basal planes [9]. By contrast, doping of heteroatoms (such as N [10], S [11], and B [12]) is an effective approach for adding active edge sites, which alter chemisorption of O2 and decrease potential in ORR, thus enhancing electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials [13]. For example, Jin et al. conducted ORR research and revealed GQDs anchored on graphene nanoribbons, which exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity with high selectivity and stability in alkaline solutions [14]. Xiao et al. reported that N-GQDs attached onto reduced graphene not only led to good catalytic activity for ORR in alkaline electrolytes but also better stability and stronger durability to methanol. Covalently bonded N atoms doped in GQDs can thoroughly change their electrochemical properties and engender more active edge sites, thus promoting catalytic performance for ORR [15]. Fang et al. presented that types of N species, particularly pyridinic-N, markedly contribute to electrocatalytic ORR [16]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no attempt was proposed to systematically study the effects of doped-N types and dopant contents on electrocatalytic activity in ORR. Therefore, investigations should be focused on electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs through doping treatment and understanding of doping effects of dopant types, structure, and content on electrocatalytic process.

Herein, the N-GQD/reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) was prepared by using N-GQDs as precursors and GO as supporting material after hydrothermal treatment, which resulted in uniform distribution of N-GQDs on r-GO. Raw N-GQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal process with GQDs as primary sources and ammonia as nitrogen source. N-GQD/r-GO samples with different pyridinic-N contents were adjusted using doped volumes of ammonia in hydrothermal reaction. N-GQD/r-GO samples exhibited four-electron pathway reaction, resistance to crossover in methanol electrolyte, and long-term stability. Moreover, pyridinic-N played an important part in electrocatalytic ORR. The N-GQD/r-GO can substitute Pt/Cas a promising catalyst for use in metal–air batteries and fuel cells.

The GQDs were synthesized according to the method [17]. The N-GQDs samples (N-GQDs-50, N-GQDs-200, and N-GQDs-250, numbers denote the volume of ammonia water) were prepared by heating the mixture of GQDs and ammonia water. In short, 150mg GQDs, 250mL ammonia water and 50mL deionized water were added into 500mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained hybrid solution was filtered through microporous PTFE membrane (50mm, 0.22µm) and then dialyzed with dialysis membrane (3500MWCO) for 24h. The N-GQDs-250 were gathered from the dialyzed aqueous solution under drying.

The 4mL homogeneous GO aqueous dispersion (1mg/mL) and 1mL N-GQDs aqueous were added into 15mL deionized water and then under ultrasonication for 30min. Then the above solution was transferred into 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. This hydrothermal treatment could reduce GO and stick the N-GQDs onto the r-GO. After the hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained N-GQDs/r-GO solution was dried and saved for subsequent use.

The Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (FE-TEM) images were tested under the JEM-2100F electron microscope. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained with an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer with Al Kɑ Radiation (1486.6eV).

The Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out using the electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua CHI750E) in a standard three-electrode cell and taken from −1.0V to 0.2V at a scan rate of 5mV/s in 0.1M KOH electrolyte saturated with N2 or O2. And a Pt wire and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The ORR characterization was carried out by the rotating disk electrode (RDE) equipment (Gamry Instruments) with different rotation rates (400rpm, 900rpm, 1250rpm, 1600rpm, 2000rpm, and 2500rpm) from −1.0V to 0.2V in 0.1M KOH electrolyte. The sample solution (1mg/mL) consists of 50μL Nafion (5wt%), 450μL isopropyl alcohol and 500μL deionized water. The 6μL of the sample solution was dropped onto the glassy carbon RDE electrode. For comparison, hydrothermally prepared graphene and commercial Pt/C catalyst (20wt% platinum on carbon black) with the similar amount were studied. And the Pt/C (20wt%) was purchased from Johnson Matthey.

N-GQDs doped GOs were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. Under hydrothermal treatment, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups of N-GQDs and GO sheets underwent intramolecular and/or intermolecular dehydration, resulting in tight conjunction of N-GQD/r-GO by π–π stacking and covalent bonds [18]. Fig. 1 shows FE-TEM image of N-GQDs-250/r-GO. The image displays that granular N-GQDs-250 are decorated uniformly onto r-GO after hydrothermal treatment. N-GQDs exhibit the size distribution range from 2nm to 6nm, with an average diameter of 4.5nm (Fig. S1). N-GQDs displayed crystalline structures and plane lattice spacing of 0.21nm, which are similar to those of graphite in Fig. 1b [19,20].

XPS survey was employed to clarify elemental compositions and N species in N-GQDs. Doped-N was found at 400eV of GQDs in XPS spectra (Fig. 2 a). N-GQDs mainly contained three elements, namely, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 2a). Notably, a peak at 497eV corresponds to Na peak. Under high resolution, N 1s spectra of N-GQDs-250 was divided into two peaks at 398.8 and 399.9eV (Fig. 2b), which can be assigned to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N based on their respective binding energies [21]. Fig. S2 displays similar peak deconvolution of N 1s core level of N-GQDs-50 and N-GQDs-200.

For further studies of electrocatalytic activity of N species in ORR, ammonia volume were adjusted in preparation of samples with different doped-N contents. With addition of different ammonia volumes, N contents of different samples increased from 3.27% to 4.50% (In Table 1 , ratios of different N species are based on the equation: NX%=NX/(N1+N2)×100%, where N1 and N2 represent peak areas of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, which were both acquired from peak deconvolution of N 1s XPS spectra). Notably, contents of pyridinic-N increased as well. Pyridinic-N is an important N species for electrocatalysis of ORR despite being controversial in certain aspects [22–26]. Pyridinic-N can be the most important N species to promote onset potential of ORR because of localized lone electron pairs in N-type species [27].

ORR represents a crucial electrochemical process in metal–air batteries and is usually assessed by different parameters, such as onset potential, oxygen reduction peak, and electron transfer number [28,29]. To evaluate catalytic activity of N-GQD/r-GO, cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out in O2- or N2-saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte using conventional three-electrode electrochemical cells. As shown in Fig. 3 a, from −1.0V to +0.2V, N-GQDs/r-GO-250 showed only capacitive currents with −1.57 current density in N2-saturated electrolyte. By contrast, N-GQDs/r-GO-250 displayed a well-defined cathodic peak at −0.31 V with −1.82 current density in O2-saturated electrolyte.

The RDE measurement was carried out to reveal ORR kinetics and onset potential represents an important part of standards to assess electrocatalytic activity [30]. The onset potentials reached ca. −0.1, −0.13, −0.15, and −0.2 V for N-GQD/r-GO-250, N-GQD/r-GO-200, N-GQD/r-GO-50, and blank r-GO, respectively (Fig. 3b). N-GQDs/r-GO-250 possibly featured a pronounced electrocatalytic activity according to different onset potentials of samples, and this property can be attributed to the relative contents of pyridinic-N and numerous researchers confirmed that pyridinic-N favors alteration of onset potential in ORR [26,31,32]. During electrocatalyzed ORR, adjacent C atoms of pyridinic-N tuned atomic charges, possibly causing absorption of intermediate products, formation of the CO bond, and breaking of OO bond [33]. Special note from Fig. 3b inset, the better onset potential of N-GQD/r-GO samples was discovered resulted from increased pyridinic-N contents. The LSV of N-GQD/r-GO-250 (Fig. 3c) revealed that current density increased, contributing to promot diffusion in KOH solution with increasing rotation rate [34,35].

In order to further research on eletrocatalytic activity, the electron transfer number was calculated by using Koutecky–Levich equations [36], and the corresponding curves of N-GQD/r-GO-250 were drawn at different potentials from −0.6 V to −0.9 V versus SCE in Fig. 3c inset, which reveals a first-order reaction with respect to dissolved oxygen, as indicated by matched lines [37]. The electron transfer number of N-GQD/r-GO-250 reached 3.6–4.2 within the potential range of −0.6 V to −0.9 V, detecting a four-electron ORR. Accordingly, the electron transfer number values of N-GQD/r-GO-200 and N-GQD/r-GO-50 totaled 3.03–3.62 and 2.8–3.41, respectively (Fig. 3d). The results were clarified that N-GQD/r-GO-250 exhibited a better four-electron reaction.

O2-saturated electrolyte containing 3 M methanol (a typical fuel molecule) provided N-doped carbon materials with superior stability and tolerance opposed to those of commercial Pt/C catalysts [34,37,38]. In this study, N-GQD/r-GO-250 sample presented fundamental catalytic properties. N-GQD/r-GO-250 sample was resistant to O2-saturated electrolyte containing 3 M methanol (Fig. S3d), and cathodic oxygen reduction peak for Pt/C (20wt%) disappeared under similar testing circumstances (Fig. S3c). After 300 CV cycles, no evident decrease in current was observed in O2-saturated KOH electrolyte. This result suggests similar stable catalytic activities of N-GQD/r-GO-250 (Fig. S3b) and Pt/C (20wt%) (Fig. S3a), as noted in other papers [37,39].

Fig. 4 a revealed that the distribution of two N types for N-GQDs obtained at different volumes of ammonia. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the dependence of the average electron transfer number on ammonia volumes and pyridinic-N content, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the average electron transfer number was 3.25 and 3.44 for N-GQDs/r-GO-50 and N-GQDs/r-GO-200, respectively, while 3.97 was observed for N-GQDs/r-GO-250, respectively. Importantly, it can be found that the average n of N-GQDs/r-GO samples was virtually proportional to the pyridinic-N content (shown in Fig. 4c). Moreover, the onset potential of N-GQDs/r-GO samples was also almost proportional to the pyridinic-N content (shown in Fig. 4d). Based on these results, it can be reasonably concluded that (i) pyridinic-N can result in the high electrocatalytic performance and (ii) the average electron transfer number and the onset potential can be increased with increasing pyridinic-N content. In other words, it is possible to optimize the electrocatalytic performance of N-GQDs/r-GO samples by adjusting the pyridinic-N contents.

In summary, two facile hydrothermal progress were developed to prepare the high-yield N-GQDs with the diameter of ca. 2–6nm and the hybrid of N-GQDs/r-GO. The results show that the N-GQDs/r-GO exhibit high electrocatalytic performance, long-term stability, and resistance to methanol crossover. Moreover, the electrocatalytic performance can be optimized by tuning the pyridinic-N content of N-GQDs samples. Furthermore, the average electron transfer number and the onset potential all almost proportional to the pyridinic-N content. The metal-free catalysts will promote development of metal–air batteries and fuel cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11764011, 11364010, 51662004 & 21671129), and Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (Nos. 2016GXNSFAA380008 & 2015GXNSFBA139002), P. R. China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.08.047.
